feature: Demo: Add employee search and salary range filter

issue.title: Demo: Add employee search and salary range filter

problem_statement: The payroll dashboard currently displays all employees without any way to search or filter the list. When managing a growing roster, users need to quickly locate employees by name or job title and narrow the view to a specific salary range. Adding search and salary range filters to the existing dashboard will make the tool more practical and also demonstrate the end-to-end OpenHands + OpenSpec spec-driven workflow for a real, self-contained feature.

user_stories.i_want: to search employees by name or job title on the payroll dashboard
user_stories.so_that: I can quickly find a specific person or role without scrolling through the entire roster

user_stories.i_want: to filter employees by a minimum and maximum salary range
user_stories.so_that: I can focus on compensation bands relevant to a review or budget exercise

user_stories.i_want: to see which filters are currently active and clear them with a single action
user_stories.so_that: I always know the view is filtered and can easily return to the full roster

user_stories.i_want: to see the full spec → plan → tasks → PR workflow applied to a real payroll feature
user_stories.so_that: I can evaluate whether the OpenSpec spec-driven process works end-to-end

must_have: A text search input on the main dashboard that filters the employee list by name or job title
A minimum salary input and a maximum salary input that filter the employee list to the specified range
The employee roster table and edit cards must both reflect the active filter results
Active filters must be visually indicated (e.g., displayed as filter tags or labels above the list)
A clear/reset button that removes all active filters and returns to the full employee list
Filtering must not break any existing CRUD operations (add, update, delete employees)
The feature must work with the existing server-side rendering pattern (no framework swap required)
should_have: Filter state is preserved via query parameters so the filtered URL can be bookmarked or shared
The summary stats section (headcount, total payroll, average salary) updates to reflect filtered results when filters are active
An empty-state message is shown when no employees match the active filters
Salary range inputs accept only non-negative integers consistent with the existing salary validation
nice_to_have: Client-side live filtering without a full page reload for faster interactivity
A combined salary slider component in addition to the min/max text inputs
Highlight matching text within name or title for search results

acceptance_criteria: Entering a partial name or job title in the search input shows only employees whose name or title contains the query (case-insensitive)
Setting a minimum salary hides all employees with a salary below that value
Setting a maximum salary hides all employees with a salary above that value
Setting both min and max salary shows only employees within that inclusive range
Active filter values are displayed in the UI so users can see what filters are applied
Clicking the clear/reset button removes all filters and restores the full employee list
After adding, updating, or deleting an employee, any previously active filters are preserved
When no employees match the active filters, an appropriate empty-state message is shown instead of an empty table
Filtering by salary range does not allow a min value greater than the max value without surfacing an error or correcting the inputs
The spec.json artifact exists and passes OpenSpec schema validation (npm run validate:openspec)
out_of_scope: Saving or persisting filter presets across sessions
Advanced search operators (boolean logic, wildcards, exact phrase matching)
Filtering by fields other than name, title, and salary (e.g., manager, address)
Export of filtered results to CSV or other formats
Changes to any existing CRUD routes or database schema
Mobile-optimized or fully accessible design beyond basic semantic HTML
open_questions: Should filtering be implemented server-side (query params on GET /) or client-side (JavaScript in the browser)? Server-side is consistent with the existing pattern and allows shareable URLs; client-side would be faster but adds JavaScript complexity.

Should the summary stats section (headcount, payroll totals, averages) reflect filtered results or always show the full roster totals? Reflecting filtered results is more useful but adds rendering complexity.

Should the salary range filter use a dedicated /filter or /search route, or should it use query parameters on the existing GET / route? Query parameters on GET / are simpler and consistent with the existing message query param pattern.
Should an invalid salary range (min > max) silently swap the values, show a validation error, or simply return no results?
